Call django.contrib.auth.password_validation.validate_password() with validation functions before setting the password. See https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/3.0/topics/auth/passwords/ for more information.
[AI Deep Verify: Attack scenario not viable — The rule name mentions Django, but the code is a Flask app. The password validation rule is Django-specific and does not apply to Flask. Additionally, the code snippet only shows 'requires login' comment, not actual password setting. No concrete attack path.]

attack_scenario.explanation: The rule name mentions Django, but the code is a Flask app. The password validation rule is Django-specific and does not apply to Flask. Additionally, the code snippet only shows 'requires login' comment, not actual password setting. No concrete attack path.

findings.title: python.django.security.django-no-csrf-token.django-no-csrf-token: Manually-created forms in django templates should specify a csrf_token to prevent CSRF attacks.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.django.security.django-no-csrf-token.django-no-csrf-token detected: Manually-created forms in django templates should specify a csrf_token to prevent CSRF attacks.
findings.code_snippet: 6 | requires login

attack_scenario.actor: Any unauthenticated or authenticated attacker who can craft a malicious HTML form and trick a logged-in user into submitting it.
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker crafts a form that submits to the vulnerable endpoint (e.g., add_transaction). 2. Attacker hosts the form on an external site or embeds it in an email. 3. Victim, who is logged into the app, visits the attacker's page and submits the form. 4. The form submission includes the victim's session cookie, and the server processes the request without a CSRF token, performing the action (e.g., adding a transaction) on behalf of the victim.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can perform state-changing actions (e.g., add, edit, delete transactions) on behalf of an authenticated victim without their consent, potentially leading to data manipulation or financial loss.
attack_scenario.explanation: The form in add_transaction.html lacks a CSRF token. Since the app uses Flask (not Django), the CSRF vulnerability is real regardless of the rule name. The attacker can exploit this via cross-site request forgery.

findings.tool_output: Rule: python.django.security.django-no-csrf-token.django-no-csrf-token | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: <nil>

findings.title: html.security.audit.missing-integrity.missing-integrity: This tag is missing an 'integrity' subresource integrity attribute. The 'integrity' attribute allows for the browser to verify that externally hosted files (for example from a CDN) are delivered without unexpected manipulation. Without this attribute, if an attacker can modify the externally hosted resource, this could lead to XSS and other types of attacks. To prevent this, include the base64-encoded cryptographic hash of the resource (file) you’re telling the browser to fetch in the 'integrity' attribute for all externally hosted files.
findings.description: Semgrep rule html.security.audit.missing-integrity.missing-integrity detected: This tag is missing an 'integrity' subresource integrity attribute. The 'integrity' attribute allows for the browser to verify that externally hosted files (for example from a CDN) are delivered without unexpected manipulation. Without this attribute, if an attacker can modify the externally hosted resource, this could lead to XSS and other types of attacks. To prevent this, include the base64-encoded cryptographic hash of the resource (file) you’re telling the browser to fetch in the 'integrity' attribute for all externally hosted files.
findings.code_snippet: 7 | requires login

attack_scenario.actor: An attacker who can compromise the CDN or perform a man-in-the-middle attack to modify the externally hosted JavaScript file.
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker gains control over the CDN serving the script (e.g., via DNS hijacking, CDN compromise, or MITM). 2. Attacker modifies the script to include malicious code (e.g., XSS payload). 3. Victim loads the page, which includes the tampered script. 4. The malicious script executes in the victim's browser, stealing session tokens or performing actions on their behalf.
attack_scenario.impact: Full compromise of the victim's session and data within the application, including potential account takeover and data exfiltration.
attack_scenario.explanation: The script tag on line 7 of base.html is missing the integrity attribute. This is a real supply chain risk. The attacker can modify the CDN resource, leading to XSS.

findings.title: python.django.security.django-no-csrf-token.django-no-csrf-token: Manually-created forms in django templates should specify a csrf_token to prevent CSRF attacks.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.django.security.django-no-csrf-token.django-no-csrf-token detected: Manually-created forms in django templates should specify a csrf_token to prevent CSRF attacks.
findings.code_snippet: 5 | requires login

attack_scenario.actor: Any unauthenticated or authenticated attacker who can craft a malicious HTML form and trick a logged-in user into submitting it.
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker crafts a form that submits to the vulnerable endpoint (e.g., edit_transaction). 2. Attacker hosts the form on an external site or embeds it in an email. 3. Victim, who is logged into the app, visits the attacker's page and submits the form. 4. The form submission includes the victim's session cookie, and the server processes the request without a CSRF token, performing the action (e.g., editing a transaction) on behalf of the victim.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can modify existing transactions on behalf of an authenticated victim without their consent, potentially leading to data manipulation or financial loss.
attack_scenario.explanation: The form in edit_transaction.html lacks a CSRF token. Since the app uses Flask (not Django), the CSRF vulnerability is real regardless of the rule name. The attacker can exploit this via cross-site request forgery.

findings.tool_output: Rule: python.django.security.django-no-csrf-token.django-no-csrf-token | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: <nil>

findings.title: python.django.security.django-no-csrf-token.django-no-csrf-token: Manually-created forms in django templates should specify a csrf_token to prevent CSRF attacks.
findings.description:
Semgrep rule python.django.security.django-no-csrf-token.django-no-csrf-token detected: Manually-created forms in django templates should specify a csrf_token to prevent CSRF attacks.
[AI Deep Verify: Attack scenario not viable — The login form typically does not require CSRF protection because the user is not yet authenticated. CSRF attacks target authenticated users to perform state-changing actions. Login forms are often excluded from CSRF protection as they are used to establish a session. Additionally, the rule name mentions Django, but the app is Flask. However, even in Flask, login forms are commonly exempted. No concrete attack path.]
findings.code_snippet: 5 | requires login

attack_scenario.explanation: The login form typically does not require CSRF protection because the user is not yet authenticated. CSRF attacks target authenticated users to perform state-changing actions. Login forms are often excluded from CSRF protection as they are used to establish a session. Additionally, the rule name mentions Django, but the app is Flask. However, even in Flask, login forms are commonly exempted. No concrete attack path.

findings.tool_output: Rule: python.django.security.django-no-csrf-token.django-no-csrf-token | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: <nil>

findings.title: python.django.security.django-no-csrf-token.django-no-csrf-token: Manually-created forms in django templates should specify a csrf_token to prevent CSRF attacks.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.django.security.django-no-csrf-token.django-no-csrf-token detected: Manually-created forms in django templates should specify a csrf_token to prevent CSRF attacks.
findings.code_snippet: 5 | requires login

attack_scenario.actor: Any unauthenticated attacker who can craft a malicious registration form and trick a victim into submitting it.
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker crafts a form that submits to the registration endpoint. 2. Attacker hosts the form on an external site or embeds it in an email. 3. Victim, who may or may not be logged in, visits the attacker's page and submits the form. 4. The form submission includes the victim's session cookie (if any), and the server processes the request without a CSRF token, creating an account on behalf of the victim or with attacker-controlled data.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can create accounts without the victim's consent, potentially leading to account takeover or abuse of the application's resources.
attack_scenario.explanation: The form in register.html lacks a CSRF token. Even though registration is a state-changing action, CSRF protection is still important to prevent unwanted account creation. The rule name mentions Django, but the app is Flask; however, the vulnerability pattern is real.

findings.tool_output: Rule: python.django.security.django-no-csrf-token.django-no-csrf-token | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: <nil>

findings.title: Unauthenticated ANY endpoint: /delete_transaction/<int:transaction_id>
findings.description: The ANY /delete_transaction/<int:transaction_id> route has no visible authentication middleware. Sensitive operations should require authentication.
findings.code_snippet: 146 | @app.route('/delete_transaction/<int:transaction_id>', methods=['POST'])

attack_scenario.actor: Any unauthenticated user with network access to the application
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends a POST request to /delete_transaction/1 (or any valid transaction ID). 2. The route handler at line 146 executes without any authentication check. 3. The transaction is deleted from the database.
attack_scenario.impact: An attacker can delete any transaction record, causing data loss and potentially financial or operational harm.
attack_scenario.explanation: The route lacks any authentication decorator or manual check, allowing any unauthenticated user to delete transactions. This is a real authorization bypass vulnerability.

findings.title: Potential Hex-encoded secret (32+ hex chars as string)
findings.description:
Regex pattern detected a potential Hex-encoded secret (32+ hex chars as string) at app\__init__.py:5. This may be a hardcoded secret that automated scanners missed.
[Also reported by another step: Regex pattern detected a potential Encryption/secret key hardcoded at app\__init__.py:5. This may be a hardcoded secret that automated scanners missed.]
findings.code_snippet: 5 | app.secret_key = '1010dba9d636960e7f19d92abab8f6c9'

attack_scenario.actor: Any attacker with read access to the source code (e.g., via public repository, insider, or decompilation). No special privileges required.
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker obtains the source code (e.g., from a public GitHub repo, leaked file, or by decompiling the application). 2. Attacker reads line 5 of app/__init__.py: `app.secret_key = '1010dba9d636960e7f19d92abab8f6c9'`. 3. Attacker uses this secret key to forge Flask session cookies, decrypt existing session data, or sign arbitrary session payloads.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can forge arbitrary Flask session cookies, leading to session hijacking, privilege escalation (e.g., impersonating any user, including admin), and potentially remote code execution if session data is deserialized unsafely. The secret key is a 32-character hex string (128-bit), which is cryptographically strong but hardcoded, making it equivalent to a static password.
attack_scenario.explanation: The finding is real because the secret key is hardcoded in plaintext. Even though it appears to be a random hex string, its presence in source code means anyone with access to the code can use it to compromise session integrity. This is a classic hardcoded secret vulnerability.

findings.title: SBOM generated: 21 components detected
findings.description: Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) generated in CycloneDX format. 21 components inventoried for vulnerability tracking.

findings.title: SBOM generation available via syft
findings.description: Run 'syft scan <target> -o cyclonedx-json' to generate a Software Bill of Materials. SBOMs are required for supply chain transparency (US Executive Order 14028).

findings.title: OpenSSF Scorecard not installed
findings.description: Install OpenSSF Scorecard (https://github.com/ossf/scorecard) to get automated security health checks for your dependencies and build pipeline.
